entries.end_notes: The Greek of the Hosea passage seems to reflect a confused tradition since our MT text refers to וְלֵתֶךְ שְׂעֹרִים after already mentioning וְחֹמֶר שְׂעֹרִים. But clearly the LXX text is superior and nbl here should not be taken to be a dry measure but rather the normal reflection of the Hebrew expression נֵבֶל־יָיִן, sometimes rendered as νεβελ οἴνου, and sometimes as ἀσκὸν οἴνου. On the other hand, the interpretatin as a dry measure is an ancient guess and should be rejected.

Given that there are more than two dozen Aramaic words for different kinds of pitchers and similar vessels, one must wonder why the targumist has resorted to using the Biblical source word.  Our guess is that he did not understand the Biblical word, wondering why a "wineskin" (so usually in BH) could be made of clay.

The original meaning is based on MH usage and the noun usage of a bird's claw or spur. Later lexicographers, undoubtedly influenced by the LXX, understood it as in the original context as "to spring up."
Samaritan usage is from Arabic.

meanings.gloss: (of an animal) to dig up the ground

entries.form_section: JLA, LJLA u/a (with e/a vars.); Syr. e/u; JPA a/a !
entries.end_notes: This verb is relatively limited compared to the usage of the partial synonym ybš. Although they overlap semantically, the latter has the frequent connotation of "to wither" whereas ngb occurs primarily with the disapperance of liquid per se. Note especially Onkelos in Genesis where this verb is used for water, while ybš is used for the drying of the ground; but the author of PsJ may not have realized the proper distinction.

The translation "chased" of metals in P-S and DJPA is dubious. better "worked".
For 'to outlast by sorcery' for the itpe, cf. ngyd: 'necromancy'.
The translation "pant" in CCPA I for the Jer. 14:6 passage is simply incorrect. It is a literal translation of the Greek, which may or may not be understood metaphorically.
See also s.v. ngr.

entries.end_notes: In older texts nwr is the generic/collective "fire", used for cooking or burning, whereas ˀšh is a large, specific instance, i.e. a fiery flame. When nwr is in the plural, it refers to multiple instances of conflagrations. Archaizing dialects, such as Mandaic, tend to preserve this distinction.

entries.form_section: In OA and pre-Egyptian OfA (and Hebraizing LJLA), nṣr. Impv w.o. nun, but nun regularly appears in the OfA preformative forms as well as in LJLA Hebraizing exxs (for the latter see s.v. nṣr). The impf. in Syriac varies between a and o thematic vowels, with grammarians and lexicographers disagreeing on which is the rarer form!
entries.end_notes: Com. Sem: nżr. The gloss "to keep something fresh" in DJBA is dubious.

Attested as a corruption in PTalm. see DJPA s.v.
We disagree with DJBA 756n. s.v. (a suggestion which itself seems to conflict with what is stated there s.v. לם adv.) The suggestion to connect this word with the Syr. enclitic particle ܠܡ is perfectly reasonable, especially in light of the derivation of the latter from 'to say' as demonstrated by Kaufman in "An Assyro-Aramaic egirtu ša Šulmu." Pp. 119-27 of Essays on the Ancient Near East in Memory of Jacob Joel Finkelstein, M. de Jong Ellis, ed. (Hamden, Ct.: Archon, 1977). A literal English equivalent would thus be 'say' as a hypothetical particle. Alternatively, and perhaps preferably, connect with ˀpm,ˀm of Egyptian OfA, 'additionally, nonetheless' pronounced something like [ammĕ]. Note that נמי often occurs after such particles as אי < אין and הכי < הכין; so that the initial nun would be due to redivision of syllables. It is unlikely that the form is connected with the unique Samaritan root of the same shape meaning "part".

Fr. MIr *nāmak.

Perhaps any large, wild, usually spotted feline.
Re meaning 2: Given that one breaks these like stone stele, we assume that these are also of stone, hence perhaps a reference to the ubiquitous feline sculptures in Neo-Hittite period monumental architecture that must still have been rather noteworthy as pagan monuments in north Syria in the early centuries of our era. An internet search for "Karatepe lion images" will produce a nice sample.

entries.form_section: imv. saḇ, JBA nsyb ; Sam. also )sb , LJLA also syb
entries.end_notes:
The nearly complementary distribution of the two verbs nsy < nśˀ and nsb (as well as the absence of any obvious cognates for the latter) makes it almost certain that the latter is derived from the former with the addition of the preposition b_, as long recognized. All the more interesting, then, is the first-cited single example from OfA Ahiqar that uses the two as synonyms!
NB: Many of the idiomatic usages with object nouns are reflections of Greek originals.

The rare examples of pael in Syriac (if justified!) would all seem to be idiosyncratic usages in place of the standard meanings of afel.
That the af’el may be used with rwḥˀ for "to apologize, defend" (so LS2) is highly uncertain.

entries.form_section: Pl. nap̄šān, nap̄šāṯā. The postvocalic sequence /pš/ in this common word resulted in many phonetic variations: In OA (and even in late western Syriac: see BHGr2 205:7), nbš, in Palm. also nm$; in East Syr. pronounced /nawšā/ along with other common words with syllable-ending spirantized peh; see s.v. brt ˀnš for this possibility in LJLA.
entries.end_notes:
Sam. also nps . It is clear from the many Nabataean examples (among others), that the npš is the funeral monument bearing the memorial inscription. In the early KTMW text, however, it could be the funerary inscription or, less likely though most so think, the entombed spirit itself. Compare also np!yšh (not to be read nwyšh) in the Jauf inscription (Torrey, JAOS 54[1934]:29ff) in reference to a cemetery or memorial stone.
In OT translations one may often translate npš + pron.suff. as "one's self", but the connotation is always more "one's existence".
The earlier Syriac lexica seem to be unaware of the original meaning of this word as "throat, appetite", that is still to be found, at least as echoes, in the earliest Biblical and (possibly the poetic) texts.

entries.end_notes: We have here separated between lqṭ "to glean, gather" and nqṭ "to seize", although the former meaning is found here in JBA as well. To judge by Hebrew and Arabic, though, the former is the original form of this root, meaning "to glean". Mandaic has only a third form LGṬ (see s.v. lqṭ), and is ubiquitous in the meaning "to seize", while in JBA (and in Syriac magic bowls) the form is almost always נקט. (In Geonic texts לקט must be viewed as a Hebraism.) At which stage the two forms split from a single root or should really be seen to be two different roots is unclear.

entries.form_section: G JLA yn$y (generally as a variant of Gt!); Syr. ne$E) . In Syriac the afel perfect always (?) preserves initial nun while it is assimilated in the imperfect and participle. In other dialects assimilation appears to be inconsistent.
entries.end_notes:
Note that other than in Syriac, the Gt (DJPA incorrectly Dt!) is the more common usage for "to forget".
The use of the af'el simply as "to forget" is well established in Mandaic, but the few apparent examples in targumic texts (e.g., at Deut 32:18) are almost certainly to be treated as Gt forms with assimilated taw, since the af'el is well-established in the meaning "to make forget" in those dialects.
Syriac does have examples of Dt, rendered here "make oneself forget", but they are all in poetry and need to be seen as regular metris causa variants of Gt.

entries.form_section: OA impf. regularly with preserved initial nun, presumably yintin, etc.
entries.end_notes:
The perfect is of this verb still appears regularly in Old Aramaic and Official Aramaic. In the later dialects it is regularly suppletively used in the pre-formative patterns for the even more irregular yhb. In Syriac (and less commonly CPA as a borrowing thereof) the dative preposition is assimilated to the end of the word, yielding the new root ntl, q.v.. In Babylonian, ntn and yhb merge producing ntb (see s.v. yhb).
In all these instances, users should also consult s.v. yhb for possible semantic values.

Greek νόμος.As is the case with the Greek etymon, it is often best to combine defs. 1 and 2 and translate simply "customary law".
The Greek short vowel ο was pronounced variously in the later koine dialects, in places like α and elsewhere (especially Egypt) like υ, hence the various initial vowels in the Aramaic forms.

entries.end_notes:
MD and others derive this word from Akkadian (i.e. nabû #2), but a connection with nbḥ seems equally possible. Cf. DJPA 725b end where nbḥ pael is to make various sounds.
Etymologically nbḥ should appear without an Akkadian velar, hence nabû B could easily be the actual cognate to this root in Akkadian as well. In Akkadian, NBH does occur, but only in SB/NB texts. ( תיקו!)
The form of the noun numbia (q.v.) matching the Akkadian noun in /u/ is probably the strongest evidence for the borrowing.
